visual_checks: Open the document properties and check whether a title has been set
Verify the title is descriptive of the document content, not a filename or generic placeholder
Compare the title in document properties to the actual content of the document to confirm relevance
Check the title bar or properties panel for the presence and quality of the title metadata
pass_conditions: Document properties include a non-empty title field
The title meaningfully describes the document content or purpose
The title is distinct from the filename and provides useful context for assistive technology users
fail_conditions: Document title field is empty or missing entirely
Document title is a generic placeholder such as 'Untitled', 'Title', or 'Microsoft Word - Document1'
Document title does not describe the actual content of the document
Document title contains only the application name (e.g., 'Microsoft PowerPoint Presentation')
na_conditions: The content is not a standalone document (e.g., it is a web page evaluated under standard 2.4.2)
ne_conditions: Document properties could not be accessed or extracted for evaluation
The document format does not support a title metadata field
common_mistakes: Leaving the default application-generated title in place (e.g., 'Presentation1')
Using the filename as the title instead of writing a descriptive title
Setting a title that was accurate for an earlier version but no longer matches updated content
Forgetting to update the title after using Save As to create a new document from an existing one

examples.pass: A PDF's document properties show the title 'City of Richlands 2025 Annual Budget Report', which accurately describes the content
examples.fail: A PDF's document properties show the title 'scan_20240315_final.pdf' which is just the filename and does not describe the content
examples.partial: The document has a title set in properties but it reads 'Draft' which is vague and does not adequately describe the document's purpose or content
